translation.content: Few names in drum & bass carry as much history as Peshay. Paul Pesce came up in early rave and left fingerprints on labels like Mo' Wax, Good Looking and, most obviously, Metalheadz. By the time drum & bass was surging in the mid-'90s, he was bolted as one of the movement's most distinctive producers. Where others pushed clinical austerity or waves of dark pressure, Pesce's ear drew him to featherlight chords instead. Atmospheric drum & bass—or intelligent, as it's sometimes known today—cohered in his hands. Pesce always dovetailed beautifully with MCs like Cleveland Watkiss and Moose, and has more than his fair share of hits. From the tactile glide of "The Piano Tune" and "Miles From Home," to "The Real Thing" off Mo Wax compilation Headz 2 (the 90 BPM version is a long-time personal favourite), his productions ring out from any set they're in. To a contemporary generation, he may be best known for 1996's Studio Set, which caught alight as a prime slice of algorithm fodder in the late 2010s, racking up millions of plays on YouTube. Alongside Bailey’s Intelligent Drum & Bass from the same year, the mix has taken on a second life as a seminal document of a genre in flux. All of which made its removal from the internet, based around a spurious copyright strike, a hot concern. Although a tad more reserved than some of the scene's dominant figureheads, like Kemistry & Storm and Fabio & Grooverider, Pesce found his niche as a loyal custodian and historian of the sound. So when Studio Set went down, we offered him a crack at making something fresh—and though it's thankfully back up, the Kafkian nightmare galvanised his commitment to preserve recorded history. Known as a DJ for his dynamic way with a groove, extended blends have long been Pesce's signature. You’ll hear plenty of those on his RA Podcast, as golden-age rollers and contemporary vocal cuts push in and out for up to four minutes, painting a portrait of the genre’s vitality from someone who helped define its terms. True to form, RA.993 carries the touch of a jazz conductor and the assured cool of a veteran who's been deep in the culture for over 30 years. – Gabriel Szatan What have you been up to recently? There's been a lot going on, from finishing my new music and working with Apple for my regular series, The History of Drum & Bass. Also, as recently highlighted, I've been bringing to light that people are taking music made and released in the '90s and, decades later, registering it as their own creations under a new name. In my case, a copyright strike for a track made by Mark Pritchard and Tom Middleton in 1995 led to Studio Session being taken down due to a copyright strike by a bad actor who had re-registered the track as his own creation and had even registered it with the United States Copyright Office. [Read more about the situation here.] So while going through the years showcasing tracks that shaped the sounds of a growing drum & bass scene in my own projects, unfortunately, the main thing I've been doing recently is understanding the true nature of fraud that exists in the music industry. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? The mix was recorded at home using turntables and Serato. I like many different styles of drum & bass, classic and new, so for this mix I thought I would do a mixture of the two. A celebration of times gone by, by incorporating a mixture of some classic tracks, with up-to-date new ones and everything in between.  What are you looking forward to in the near future? Primarily, releasing my new double album, but before that, we have some other new releases planned. However, I think the main focus at the moment is raising awareness to what’s going on in the music industry regarding fraudulent activities and copyright issues. After what has just happened, it makes you realise that the systems that are in place to protect us as artists, well, they don’t do that at all in the digital world. Even though my Studio Set has been reinstated on YouTube, there is nothing to suggest this won’t happen again going forwards. So until reforms can be made to the way copyright claims are handled online, we'll be stuck in this never-ending cycle where the same thing can keep happening. And this can happen to anyone, in any genre, at any time. This is a serious problem that needs to be looked at, because we can’t have people stealing other people’s music, changing the name, re-registering it as their own, and then releasing it, to ultimately earn money from it. And even worse than that, these bad actors are changing history by erasing the original writer of the track and replacing it with the fraudulent one. As we move forwards, how can writers, producers, composers, musicians and everybody in between, be totally confident that their music and legacy are safe online? What's one social cause you want the world to pay attention to? My focus has always been on the music, preserving its integrity and ensuring artists can thrive in an industry that respects their craft. I’m channelling my energy into re-defining how we honour musical legacies. Let’s all push for reforms that protect creativity, so the next generation of artists, regardless of genre, can innovate without barriers. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Thats a tough question. There have been many over the years. But, if I had to pick a club night, it would have to be the 'Sunday Sessions' held by Metalheadz at Blue Note in the '90s. It was special for so many different reasons. DJing there was of course a great experience, especially my comeback set in August 1995 after a long layoff with illness. I’ll remember that night forever. But also, just being there, standing in the crowd and listening to all the other DJs play. They all brought their own style and signature, it very special moment in time and is now the stuff of legend. And one party that has always stuck with me, is Liquidroom in Tokyo. I was touring Japan, Australia and New Zealand in 2000 with MC Moose. They were all great, positive audiences. But I’ll always remember the sheer energy in the Liquidroom, it was absolutely electric with people really having a great time.
translation.blurb: A new studio set from one of the foundational icons of drum & bass.
